chunk_summary: Therapeutic feeding for complicated SAM involves transitioning from starter (F-75) to catch-up (F-100) diets based on clinical criteria like edema resolution, appetite return, and medical stability, with careful monitoring of feeding tolerance and electrolyte protocols.
learning_objectives: Understand the phased approach to therapeutic feeding in complicated SAM, including starter and catch-up diets.
Identify clinical criteria for transitioning between stabilization, transition, and rehabilitation phases.
Learn the role of micronutrient and electrolyte supplementation in SAM management.
Recognize the importance of monitoring feeding tolerance and addressing risk signals.
key_facts: Starter diet (F-75 equivalent) is given at 130 mL/kg/day, reduced to 100 mL/kg/day in cases of gross edema.
Transition phase typically lasts 2–3 days and begins with loss of edema, appetite return, and medical stability.
Catch-up diet (F-100 equivalent) is introduced gradually while maintaining initial total volume to avoid osmotic load.
Rehabilitation starts when >90% of feeds are reliably finished, edema is resolved, and no major medical issues persist.
Potassium supplementation (3–4 mEq/kg/day for at least 2 weeks) and magnesium replacement are essential.
reference_quote: Therapeutic feeding in complicated SAM is staged because early metabolic instability makes rapid 'catch-up' feeding unsafe. National facility guidance aligns starter and catch-up diets with F-75 and F-100 composition and provides weight-based volumetric charts.
document_key_topics: Electrolyte and micronutrient supplementation in SAM
